author: @mattmill30
text:
I am trying to create a 7S6P Li-ion battery pack from disposable e-cigarettes and retrofit a replacement for a 2S Lead-acid e-scooter. However, the only way I can fit 6P is 2x3P end-to-end (either shared negative rail with two positive rails or shared positive rail with two negative rails), so I was hoping for some design suggestions and terminology, particularly around safety considerations.
I've previously seen a battery, either from a children's toy or a tool, with two female terminal receptacles on one edge and one female terminal receptacle on the parallel edge. But what would this terminal configuration be identified as? e.g. The Makita BL1830-PK3 battery has "sixteen firm holding contact terminals", but how would that be presented in notation? e.g. 7S6P instead of "42 cells"

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Not a bad project. I do need to comment on you energy definition. 4 Ah is not energy. You need to multiply with the voltage to get the energy contained. It's really simple. P = U * I. E=P * T. Where P is in Watt, U is in V, I is in Amps, T is in seconds and E is in Joules which is the same as a WattSecond. I hope you don't mind I point this out to you, I feel it's kind of important as the same mistake is made in nummerous articles or even in technical reports which is kind of a pain ( usually mixing up power and energy ) . If it's not clear ask your dad. He can tell you all about it.
And it's so great you spotwelded the batteries. Some people just solder them which is a big mistake potentially destroying isolation inside( for lithium batteries I'm sure of that. for others probably also. )

author: @hempev
text:
Take care with charge balance on NiMH (as with NiCd). Voltage is not the most accurate measure of % charge. Hybrid cars use single strings of NiMH for this reason; if you parallel them, you may end up with different current into *each* string. 

Or just make a single string at a higher voltage - depends on what you use as a controller or have for charging.

author: @gilbertsprojects2954
text: Nice one Luke, maybe a future upgrade could be to use lithium cells from an old laptop battery?.. 👍🏼

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text:
That is not a good idea. Lithium cells in laptops do not contain protection circuitry. Packs for laptops do. This will protect against over temperature and over current. Single cells with this protection circuitry do exist but are virtually never found in laptop battery packs.
Only if you do include the protection circuitry and you know what you are doing it is safe.  Imagen a short circuit and the pack catches fire close to some private parts you care a lot about.

replies.author: @gilbertsprojects2954
replies.text: @hanslepoeter5167 yes you’re correct, I meant use the cells with a charging/protection circuit, readily available from sites like AliExpress/eBay etc. I should have stipulated that 👍🏼

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @gilbertsprojects2954 Yes you can buy these. But they will not match the 16450 format as these need some space for protection. But thats a great comment. It indicates you know what you are talking about.

author: @totherarf
text:
Nice to see a new way of explaining it! Well done Luke ;0)
Most people would have gone with the hosepipe analogy, it is good to see someone try something different!

The only thing I would say is that Amps in battery terms is totally different from AmpHours. 
Amps is how much energy you can safely take out at any time.
AmpHours is the total amount of energy stored.
..... of course you need to add in the voltage multiplier to get the actual Power of the unit!

author: @ecospider5
text:
So we need to be careful when talking about Amp hours vs amps. You were describing Amp hours and said just amps a couple times. 

Amp hours are how long it will last. Amps are an instantaneous thing about how much wattage a battery can output right now. So at 18v hopefully you are getting at least 5.5A so you can be pushing the motor at 100w.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Hey Luke maybe you could explain why you chose to may 2 batteries of full voltage instead of one by paralleling the individual cells which may give you a more stable battery Cheers and keep up the good videos.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hi mate, Great question. my reason is that I needed more voltage on the packs to run the motor controller and the motor its self. So connecting 4 batteries into series at 3.6v each, I managed should get a higher voltage(12-16v), then connecting both packs into parallel iI should increase the amp hours from 4 to 8. I'm open to suggestions so let me know what you think. Thanks mate! :)

author: @charlesdickens6706
text: ......motor is 200 watts is it ?.The batteries could almost be from the 20 watt led emergency lights that Rob salvaged cos they can also be configured to 28 volts nominal for 4ah .The 28 volts is the turn on voltage required by the salvaged led array . I'm noting what you do for your build cos I've decided to finish off my twenty year old mothballed build . I have several 100 watt motors in the style of your motor, I'll look at using my lithium power tool battery packs paralleled up as the battery . Those lithium battery packs didn't exist twenty years ago .

author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
text: For some reason seeing those batteries chained together reminds me of a water fight between the men and the women. The women occupied the second floor of the building keeping us men pinned down on the ground floor. I decided we needed a distraction and asked the men for their Nextel phone batteries. At 4.5 volts each one alone couldn't light a 24 volt fog light, but daisy chained together using 5 or 6 batteries(ran in series) I had plenty of voltage. I added a flasher relay in series with the light. Pointed the light out the back door and turned it on. Sure enough those women got curious what the bright flashing light was and all went towards the back, in the mean time the men went running up to the third floor. FYI the flasher relay acts like a thermal limiter on the system. I still remember being told a pair of 100 watt light bulbs were cheaper to replace than 200 watt speakers. Basically saying put something inline to burn up before the motor or batteries that is cheaper to replace.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Never seen batteries like that before, very cool. Are you going to use a BMS to manage the cells? I would imagine that a standard 18650 lithium based BMS would work with these batteries because they both hold about the same voltage. What is the charge of a flat cell with those batteries, is it around 3v like the standard lithium cells?
Looking forward to seeing you scoot around the carpark when this is done.

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: ....so it looks like each tube is composed of 6 C size 2ah NiMH cells at nominal 1.2 volts per cell which would give 7.2 volts for each tube .

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @charlesdickens6706 I thought Luke said each tube was about 4v, having 4 in series gives him the 16v each pack so a standard 4S BMS would work well.

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: ......voltage readings will be up and down all over the place. Freshly charged reading could hover around say 1.45 per cell for several days depending on manufacturing, settled voltage is say a nominal 1.25 but seem to be higher until partially drained , charging voltage reading could well touch on 18 volts for 12 cells in series depending on internal resistance and charger .
